problemTitle: Minimum Number of Operations to Sort a Binary Tree by Level

sources.reviewedCode:
# Time:  O(nlogn)
# Space: O(w)

class TreeNode(object):
    # [Reader note] Entry point
    # Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Traverse first, isolate one level at a time, then solve the smaller reordering problem that level creates.
    def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
        pass


# bfs, sort
class Solution(object):
    def minimumOperations(self, root):
        """
        :type root: Optional[TreeNode]
        :rtype: int
        """
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Separating traversal from per-level processing, converting tree state into arrays, and solving the right subproblem per level.
        result = 0
        q = [root]
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        while q:
            new_q = []
            for node in q:
                # [Reader note] Decision point
                # Pay attention here: It is tempting to treat the whole tree as one sorting job, but the structure only asks you to solve smaller level-sized jobs independently.
                if node.left:
                    new_q.append(node.left)
                if node.right:
                    new_q.append(node.right)
            idx = range(len(q))
            idx.sort(key=lambda x: q[x].val)
            for i in xrange(len(q)):
                while idx[i] != i:
                    idx[idx[i]], idx[i] = idx[i], idx[idx[i]]
                    result += 1
            q = new_q
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return result

sources.relativePath: solution/2400-2499/2471.Minimum Number of Operations to Sort a Binary Tree by Level/Solution.py
sources.reviewedCode:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Traverse first, isolate one level at a time, then solve the smaller reordering problem that level creates.
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
# [Reader note] State setup
# This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Separating traversal from per-level processing, converting tree state into arrays, and solving the right subproblem per level.
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right
class Solution:
    def minimumOperations(self, root: Optional[TreeNode]) -> int:
        def swap(arr, i, j):
            arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]

        def f(t):
            n = len(t)
            # [Reader note] Main pass
            # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
            m = {v: i for i, v in enumerate(sorted(t))}
            for i in range(n):
                t[i] = m[t[i]]
            ans = 0
            for i in range(n):
                while t[i] != i:
                    swap(t, i, t[i])
                    ans += 1
            return ans

        q = deque([root])
        ans = 0
        while q:
            t = []
            for _ in range(len(q)):
                node = q.popleft()
                t.append(node.val)
                # [Reader note] Decision point
                # Pay attention here: It is tempting to treat the whole tree as one sorting job, but the structure only asks you to solve smaller level-sized jobs independently.
                if node.left:
                    q.append(node.left)
                if node.right:
                    q.append(node.right)
            ans += f(t)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return ans

writeups.title: 2471.Minimum-Number-of-Operations-to-Sort-a-Binary-Tree-by-Level
writeups.excerpt: ### 2471.Minimum-Number-of-Operations-to-Sort-a-Binary-Tree-by-Level 将属于同一个level的数字都收集起来。然后用Indexing sort的方法去贪心地交换。 比如说，对于一个乱序的nums数组，我们可以提前知道每个数字的期望位置rank[nums[i]]。我们就从前往后查看每一个位置，如果当前的`rank[nums[i]]!=i`，那么就把nums[i]与位于rank[nums[i]]的数字交换。这样的交换可以持续多次，直至我们在i这个位置上迎来期望的数字。 为什么一定能够迎来期望的数字呢？因为每一次交换，我们都把一个数字送到了它应该在的位置。一旦把n-1个数字都放到了它们对应期望的位置，那么i这个位置的数字一定也已经安排到了期望的数字。
